mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of user-defined exceptions in C++?

options.text: To replace standard library exceptions completely
To handle application-specific error conditions that standard exceptions cannot adequately describe
To make programs run faster by avoiding exception handling overhead
To prevent compilation errors in large programs

mcqs.explanation: User-defined exceptions allow programmers to represent application-specific error conditions beyond what standard exceptions offer.

mcqs.question: What is the correct syntax to define a custom exception class inheriting from std::exception?

options.text: class MyException : public std::exception { };
class MyException inherits std::exception { };
class MyException : public exception { };
class MyException extends std::exception { };

mcqs.explanation: User-defined exceptions should inherit publicly from std::exception to integrate with the standard exception hierarchy.

options.text: catch (InvalidAgeException e) { cout << e.getAge(); }
catch (InvalidAgeException& e) { cout << e.getAge(); }
catch (InvalidAgeException *e) { cout << e->getAge(); }
catch (std::runtime_error& e) { cout << e.what(); }

mcqs.explanation: Catching by reference (InvalidAgeException&) avoids object slicing and is the recommended practice for exception handling.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is considered a best practice for user-defined exceptions?

options.text: Always throw primitive types like int or char for better performance
Override the what() virtual function to provide meaningful error messages
Create separate exception classes for each possible error code
Never use inheritance when defining custom exceptions

mcqs.explanation: Overriding the what() method provides descriptive error messages that help in debugging and logging.

mcqs.question: What does 'exception safety' guarantee mean in C++?

options.text: Exceptions are never thrown during program execution
Programs maintain valid state and no resource leaks occur when exceptions are thrown
All exceptions are caught and handled immediately
The program runs faster when exception handling is used

mcqs.explanation: Exception safety ensures that when an exception occurs, the program remains in a valid state without resource leaks.

options.text: To make the function throw exceptions
To indicate the function promises not to throw exceptions, enabling better optimization
To automatically catch all exceptions within the function
To compile the program faster

mcqs.explanation: Marking what() as noexcept tells the compiler the function won't throw, allowing for better code optimization.

mcqs.question: Which exception safety level guarantees either complete success or no state changes?

options.text: Basic guarantee
No guarantee
Strong guarantee
Nothrow guarantee

mcqs.explanation: The strong guarantee ensures that if an operation fails, the program state remains unchanged from before the operation.

mcqs.question: What is the recommended approach for organizing exception hierarchies in large C++ applications?

options.text: Create a single flat exception class containing all error codes as member variables
Use a hierarchy where common exceptions share a base class, allowing catch blocks to handle groups of related exceptions
Avoid creating custom exceptions and use only std::exception for all errors
Make all exception classes standalone without any inheritance relationship

mcqs.explanation: A hierarchical exception design allows catching related exceptions at appropriate levels, promoting flexible error handling.
